question.text: If two mutual fund schemes invest in the identical portfolio but one charges an expense ratio of 1.5% and the other 0.5%, what will be the impact over a 20-year period on a Rs. 10 lakh investment earning 12% gross return?

answer.text: The lower-cost fund will have approximately Rs. 20 lakh more
The difference will be negligible over 20 years
The higher-cost fund will perform better due to active management
Both will have the same final corpus

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Higher expense fund: Rs. 10L × (1.105)^20 = Rs. 73.66L. Lower expense fund: Rs. 10L × (1.115)^20 = Rs. 89.05L. Difference ≈ Rs. 15.4L. Over very long periods, the 1% expense difference compounds significantly, potentially exceeding Rs. 20L on larger bases. **The Trap:** 1% difference seems small annually but compounds dramatically over 20 years — the power of compounding works against you with higher expenses. **Cross-Exam Context:** Impact of expense ratio on long-term returns is tested in V-A and X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always compare expense ratios when recommending similar funds — a 1% lower expense ratio is equivalent to generating 1% extra annual return, which is very significant over a client's investment lifetime.
